FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes cross-sectional data from 2-year follow-up of U.S. ABCD Study (Year 2, 2018–2020, N = 10,258 from 21 U.S. sites)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: modified Poisson regression examining associations between cyberbullying victimization/perpetration and eating disorder symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone does not qualify per binding clarification §2.4. Paper assesses cyberbullying ("Have you ever been cyberbullied...online, in texts, or group texts, or on social media") as independent variable paired with eating disorder symptoms. No separate phone/SM-specific measurement (time on phone, time on named platforms, SMAQ, MPIQ, or other phone/SM variables) is reported. Cyberbullying qualifies only when paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. Other-side variables (eating disorder symptoms) are individual-trait-level, but the phone/SM variable is missing.

notes: Paper focuses on cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as exposures to eating disorder symptoms, not on smartphone or social media use as an exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper uses Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort, a Dutch longitudinal study, not the U.S. ABCD Study. §2.1 states NOT_MET applies to 'Different cohort sharing the "ABCD" acronym—including Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD)'.

notes: Incorrect ABCD cohort. Despite title and methods mentioning problematic social media use (PSMU) and problematic gaming (PG), the underlying data source is the Dutch Amsterdam ABCD study, not the U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC1.evidence: Secondary data analysis of U.S. ABCD Study baseline (2016–2018, ages 9–10 years) through 2-year follow-up; N = 8,085 participants from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: regression models examining associations between cumulative ACEs, bullying victimization, and mental health problems (internalizing and externalizing)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Youth and Adolescence, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines ACEs and bullying victimization as predictors of mental health outcomes. Neither ACEs nor bullying victimization is a smartphone/social media variable. No phone/SM-specific measurement (time on phone, time on named platforms, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears anywhere in the paper. The analysis is ACEs + bullying → mental health, not phone/SM-based. Per binding clarifications, cyberbullying alone fails IC2 unless paired with separately-measured phone/SM variable; here cyberbullying is measured but no phone/SM variable is present. FT-IC2 requires a qualifying smartphone/SM variable paired with individual-trait-level outcome.

notes: Paper addresses cumulative adversity (ACEs + bullying) and mental health outcomes. Bullying is measured as dichotomous victimization (cyberbullying and traditional bullying combined) but no phone/SM exposure variable is present.
